# Marrow

> Marrow is persistent, shared memory for AI agents — so they stop forgetting and can work together. It keeps every memory as a plain markdown file but adds what a database gives you (validation, query, hybrid search, provenance) plus three things file-based memory lacks: staleness detection (a memory is flagged the moment the code it describes changes), consolidation (duplicates merged, contradictions resolved automatically), and a coordination plane so many agent sessions share one brain instead of colliding. It runs as a single offline binary on the user's own machine, speaks the Model Context Protocol, and writes a tamper-evident audit ledger.

This file is written so an AI agent can install and use Marrow on the user's behalf. Follow the steps literally.

## Install

```bash
# With Rust:
cargo install --git https://github.com/aryawidjaja/marrow marrow-cli marrow-mcp
# Or without Rust (prebuilt binaries):
#   curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aryawidjaja/marrow/main/install.sh | sh

marrow init                                # creates .marrow/ in the current project
```

Verify: `marrow status` should print `total: 0`.

## Connect Marrow to this agent (Claude Code)

Best — one command from the project dir; registers the MCP server (all projects), installs the
auto-capture hooks, and adds a CLAUDE.md guidance block, then restart Claude Code:
```
marrow setup            # this project; add --global to wire every project at once
```

Tools only (no hooks), or other agents:
- Claude Code, every project: `claude mcp add marrow -s user -- marrow-mcp --root .`
- Per-project / Cursor — `.mcp.json` (or `.cursor/mcp.json`):
  `{"mcpServers":{"marrow":{"command":"marrow-mcp","args":["--root","."]}}}`
- Codex (TOML): `[mcp_servers.marrow]` with `command = "marrow-mcp"`, `args = ["--root","."]`.

After connecting, the agent gains the `mem_*` tools below. (`--root .` = each project keeps its own
memory under `.marrow/`, created on first use.)

## Onboarding an existing repo (do this once)

A fresh store is empty. If the project already has docs, seed memory from them so future sessions
start warm: call `mem_ingest` (or run `marrow ingest`) to get the list of knowledge docs, then read
each and `mem_write` the durable decisions/facts — distill, don't paste whole files; `mem_recall`
first to skip anything already saved. `mem_bootstrap` will nudge you to do this when the brain is
empty but docs exist.

## How an agent should use Marrow (the loop)

1. **Start warm.** Call `mem_bootstrap` with your goal. You get back: the project's AREAS (its table of
   contents), what other sessions are doing, the memories most relevant to your goal, and any memories
   whose code has since CHANGED. Do not re-scan the codebase first.
2. **Recall before you answer.** For any question about how this project works or what was decided,
   call `mem_recall`. It returns the matches AND the memories connected to them, in one call. The
   connected ones carry `hops`: `1` is directly linked to a match, `2` or more means the brain
   followed the chain to reach it. A high-`hops` memory did not match your words at all, so read it —
   it is often the thing you did not know to ask for.
3. **Remember what you learn.** The moment you reach a durable decision, fact, or gotcha, call
   `mem_write`. Three things matter:
   - `area`: which part of the project this belongs to (`auth`, `billing`). Call `mem_areas` first and
     REUSE an existing area. If nothing fits, leave it out; an unfiled memory is still searchable, a
     misfiled one is a lie.
   - `topic`: a SHORT LABEL, never a sentence. It is the key the brain de-duplicates by, and a new
     memory on the same topic supersedes the old one. This is what stops the brain growing forever.
   - `[[links]]`: reference related memories by id or topic in the body. Links are how an old memory
     stays findable once nobody searches for its words any more, so link generously.
   Optional but worth it: `confidence` when you are NOT sure (an uncertain memory is useful, a
   confidently wrong one is not), and `expires_at` for something only true for now, which Marrow
   retires on its own.
4. **Anchor what is about code.** If the memory describes how a specific function or type behaves, pass
   `anchor: {file, symbol}` to `mem_write`. Marrow fingerprints that symbol and warns the next session
   the moment the code changes, instead of confidently telling it something stale.
5. **Supersede, do not duplicate.** When a decision changes, `mem_supersede` the old one. The lineage is
   kept, so you can always see what you used to think and why it changed.

You do NOT manage work-claims yourself. Claiming files, releasing them, and recording progress are done
FOR you by the Claude Code hooks. Do not call claim/release tools; they are not in your tool list.

## Tools (MCP) and CLI equivalents


Warm start: `mem_bootstrap`/`marrow bootstrap`. Collision-avoidance, progress and claims are handled by the hooks; agents do not call them.

Onboarding: `mem_ingest`/`marrow ingest` (seed memory from existing docs). A `save` MCP prompt (Claude Code: `/mcp__marrow__save`, or the `/marrow-save` slash command) tells the agent to distill the current session into memory.

## Optional: hands-free auto-capture (Claude Code)

Copy `integrations/claude-code/hooks/*.sh` to `.claude/hooks/`, `chmod +x` them, and merge `integrations/claude-code/settings.example.json` into `.claude/settings.json`. Then sessions bootstrap warm, file edits stream as progress, and sessions close out automatically — no tokens spent.

## Notes

- Search is keyword (FTS5) by default. Semantic (meaning-based) search is opt-in and needs an embedding model: install with `--features embed-fastembed` (local) or `--features embed-http`, then `marrow embed fastembed` (or `marrow embed http --url <endpoint>`). `marrow status` shows the active mode.
- Everything runs locally; nothing leaves the machine. Markdown under `.marrow/memory/` is the source of truth; the SQLite index is rebuildable with `marrow doctor`.
- Watch it in a browser: `marrow-serve --root . --port 8088`.
- Full integration guide: `integrations/README.md`. Project overview: `README.md`.
